01
Minds that made money · 09
He didn't beat the market with a new idea.
He beat it by copying the best one.
An Indian-American who cloned Buffett's method, added a checklist — and out-returned him for a decade.
Meet the shameless copycat →
02
Who
Mohnish
Pabrai
b. 1964, Mumbai. Founder of Pabrai Investment Funds — a Buffett-style value fund. Sold his IT firm for ₹15+ crore, then started copying.
+1,204%
his fund's net return, 2000–2018 — vs the S&P 500's +159%.
Bio: Wikipedia. Sold TransTech (2000) for ~$20M ≈ ₹15+ crore at the time — illustrative conversion. AUM ~$900M–$1B+ (range, by source/date). Return figures: see slide 3. Sources: Wikipedia; William Green, "Richer, Wiser, Happier."

04
“
I'm a shameless copycat. Everything in my life is cloned. I have no original ideas.
Mohnish PabraiThe edge wasn't invention. It was the humility to copy the best — and the patience to wait.
What most people miss →
05
"+1,204%" sounds like a straight line up.
It wasn't. The number hides a −65% middle.
In 2008 his concentrated bets fell roughly 60–65% — worse than the market. The picks are easy to copy. The stomach to sit through that isn't.
06
One question
Could you sit through your portfolio falling 65% — without selling a thing?
That, not the stock pick, is what you'd actually be copying.
Save this for the next time your hands get itchy.
07
He didn't out-think the market.
He out-copied it — then out-sat everyone.
Now you've got
AI on your side.

Education only. Not investment advice. Figures rounded and window-specific. Sources: William Green, "Richer, Wiser, Happier"; TIME; NBC News; Wikipedia; Quartr; Fortune; GuruFocus.

Caption — Instagram post body

He calls himself a "shameless copycat." Not as a confession. As a strategy.

Mohnish Pabrai didn't try to out-think Warren Buffett. He copied the method wholesale, ran a Buffett-style partnership, and from 2000 to 2018 his fund returned +1,204% net while the S&P 500 did +159%. For one long stretch, the copy beat the original. Then in 2007 he paid ₹5+ crore to literally eat lunch with the man whose playbook he'd lifted.

Here's the part everyone skips. In 2008 the same concentrated bets that made him fell roughly 60–65% — worse than the market — and he spent years after 2018 lagging. The +1,204% is real, but it's window-tagged. Cherry-pick a different decade and the story flips.

So the lesson isn't "cloning prints money." It's that copying the picks is the easy 5%. Copying the stomach to sit through a 65% drawdown with your own money on the line — that's the 95% nobody clones. Most people copy the 13F and skip the temperament. Then they buy after the run, not before.

You don't need an original idea to do well. You need the patience to not flinch. That part was never for sale at the auction.

Save this for the next time the market drops and your hands get itchy. Share it with the friend who copies hot picks but not the holding period. Follow @hellojarvis_ind for more.

Education only. Not investment advice. Sources: William Green, "Richer, Wiser, Happier"; TIME; NBC News; Wikipedia; Quartr; Fortune (Sept 2025); GuruFocus.
